video.title: Every House Burned. The Painting Never Did.

character_bible.name: The Crying Boy
character_bible.description: A painted toddler boy in a heavy gilt frame, round tearful face, enormous glossy brown eyes with one bright tear on the cheek, neat side-parted brown hair, little brown jacket, warm oil-paint glow against a near-black background

character_bible.name: The Firefighter
character_bible.description: A 1980s British firefighter, dark double-breasted tunic with silver buttons, yellow helmet, soot-streaked square jaw, tired kind eyes
character_bible.palette: coal black, brass yellow, soot grey

scenes.narration: The house burned to the studs. The painting didn't even blister.
scenes.image_prompt: A gutted burned kitchen, blackened skeleton beams, and one pristine gilt-framed painting still hanging square on the charred wall, empty room

scenes.narration: A little boy, painted in oils, crying.
scenes.image_prompt: The Crying Boy painting in extreme close-up, huge glossy tearful eyes, one bright tear rolling down his cheek, heavy gilt frame

scenes.narration: Fifty thousand British homes hung him over the fireplace.
scenes.image_prompt: A dark wall of identical crying boy prints receding into blackness like a gallery of small watching faces

scenes.narration: House after house caught fire. The boy always survived.
scenes.image_prompt: A brick terraced street at night, one house vomiting flame and smoke, every other window dark, deserted street

scenes.narration: Firefighters began keeping count.
scenes.image_prompt: The Firefighter dragging a chalk tally mark onto a soot-black brick wall, helmet under one arm

scenes.narration: One officer stopped counting at fifty.
scenes.image_prompt: Close-up of the Firefighter's tired soot-streaked face, eyes narrowed at something off-frame

scenes.narration: This is the exhibit nobody could burn.
scenes.image_prompt: The crying boy painting alone on a velvet museum stand under one hard spotlight, deep darkness all around, empty hall

scenes.narration: Why wouldn't the Crying Boy burn?
scenes.image_prompt: A yellow fire helmet resting on scorched floorboards, the crying boy painting reflected upside down in its visor

scenes.narration: Rotherham, England. 1985. Ron and May Hall's kitchen. One chip pan.
scenes.image_prompt: A modest 1980s British kitchen at night, a chip pan on the stove beginning to flare orange, empty room

scenes.narration: The fire ate the kitchen, the carpet, the ceiling.
scenes.image_prompt: Fire rolling across a living-room ceiling in a single wave, furniture silhouetted black beneath it, nobody around

scenes.narration: When the smoke cleared, one face was still watching. Untouched.
scenes.image_prompt: The Crying Boy painting hanging perfectly on a destroyed black wall, thin smoke curling past his bright tear

scenes.narration: Ron's brother fought fires for a living. He wasn't surprised.
scenes.image_prompt: The Firefighter standing in the ruined doorway staring at the painting, hose hanging slack in his hand

scenes.narration: His station had pulled that same print from ruin after ruin.
scenes.image_prompt: A row of soot-blackened crying boy prints leaning against a fire-station wall in lamplight

scenes.narration: Then a newspaper made him famous. Front page. The curse of the Crying Boy.
scenes.image_prompt: A towering stack of fresh newspapers on a dark press-room floor, bold black headline blocks suggested without readable letters, deserted

scenes.narration: The phones didn't stop ringing for a week.
scenes.image_prompt: A 1980s office telephone knocked off its hook, receiver swinging on its cord under a harsh desk lamp, empty desk

scenes.narration: Kilburn. Nottingham. Surrey. Different houses, different fires. Always the boy.
scenes.image_prompt: Three burned-out houses displayed in a row like museum dioramas, one small bright painting glowing unharmed in each

scenes.narration: Then the backstory arrived, the way legends always do.
scenes.image_prompt: An old leather suitcase spilling dried roses and yellowed papers across a dark table, one candle, empty room

scenes.narration: The story goes: a painter found the boy in Madrid. An orphan.
scenes.image_prompt: A painter's easel in a sun-bleached Spanish courtyard, a small sad boy posing on a wooden stool

scenes.narration: Fire followed the child. His parents had died in one.
scenes.image_prompt: A small boy silhouetted before a burning farmhouse at night, clutching a blanket

scenes.narration: Villagers crossed the street when he passed. Devil-touched, they whispered.
scenes.image_prompt: Villagers under a swinging lantern turning away from a small boy standing alone in the street

scenes.narration: He died at nineteen, they say. In a burning car.
scenes.image_prompt: A vintage car engulfed in flame on a dark empty country road, deserted

scenes.narration: None of it checks out. No records. No orphanage. No boy.
scenes.image_prompt: An empty records-office shelf with one drawer pulled out and hollow, dust drifting in lamplight, deserted

scenes.narration: The painter was real, though. Bruno Amadio, selling sorrow to tourists by the crate.
scenes.image_prompt: A cramped Venice studio crowded with dozens of crying-child paintings stacked floor to ceiling

scenes.narration: Here's the twist: there was never one Crying Boy. There was a catalogue.
scenes.image_prompt: Twenty-eight subtly different crying children portraits arranged in a tight grid on a dark gallery wall

scenes.narration: Halloween, 1985. The newspaper announced its solution: a bonfire.
scenes.image_prompt: An unlit towering bonfire stack at dusk, gilt picture-frame corners jutting from the woodpile, deserted field

scenes.narration: Readers mailed in their paintings by the sackful. Thousands of them.
scenes.image_prompt: Bulging grey mail sacks slumped against a loading dock, corners of gilt frames poking out of them, empty dock

scenes.narration: Sacks of crying children went into the flames.
scenes.image_prompt: The bonfire raging against the night, picture frames blackening and curling in the blaze, sparks climbing high

scenes.narration: The paper declared the curse dead. The reports kept coming.
scenes.image_prompt: A cold heap of bonfire ash at dawn with one gilt frame corner unburned in the grey, deserted field

scenes.narration: Twenty-five years later, a radio detective took the question to a fire lab.
scenes.image_prompt: A clinical fire-research laboratory, steel table and instruments, one crying boy print waiting under cold lights

scenes.narration: They put a genuine print to the torch.
scenes.image_prompt: A gloved hand holding a blowtorch flame to the corner of the framed print

scenes.narration: The cord burned through in seconds. The painting fell. Face down.
scenes.image_prompt: The painting caught mid-fall against blackness, its face tipping toward the floor

scenes.narration: Fire-retardant varnish. A cheap factory coating, tougher than any curse.
scenes.image_prompt: Extreme close-up of flame licking across a glossy varnished surface and dying into threads of smoke

scenes.narration: Every miracle was just a painting lying face-down under its own lacquer.
scenes.image_prompt: The print lying face-down on scorched floorboards, its plain hardboard back unburned amid dying embers

scenes.narration: The boy still surfaces in charity shops. Two pounds each.
scenes.image_prompt: A cluttered thrift-shop shelf, the crying boy print propped between chipped teapots under flickering fluorescent light

scenes.narration: Buyers still turn him to face the wall.
scenes.image_prompt: The painting hung backwards on floral wallpaper, its wire and brown-paper backing facing the room, empty room

scenes.narration: The only fire that ever beat the Crying Boy was lit by a newspaper.
scenes.image_prompt: The bonfire's last ember glowing inside a charred gilt frame on black ground, deserted
